abstract: Imbalanced data pose a persistent challenge in binary classification, particularly in healthcare, where the accurate detection of minority outcomes is critical. This study investigated the effect of resampling techniques on the classification performance across four healthcare datasets with varying imbalance ratios. Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and XGBoost were evaluated under the original and resampled conditions using ROSE and SMOTE. Performance was measured using Recall, F1 Score, Balanced Accuracy, Matthews Correlation Coefficient, and curve-based evaluations, including ROC and precision-recall analyses. The results demonstrate that resampling enhances minority detection, although the improvements vary according to the dataset and classifier. ROSE occasionally benefits ensemble models, whereas SMOTE provides steadier but moderate gains. These findings emphasize that no single resampling method is optimal. Instead, careful selection tailored to the dataset and classifier is essential, underscoring the practical importance of imbalance-aware modeling in improving the reliability of medical decision support systems.

title: Fast Evaluation Method of Sonar Detection Efficiency Based on DNN
authors: Shizhe Wang, Weiyi Chen, Zongji Li, Xu Chen
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: In response to the challenge that traditional static efficiency evaluation modeling methods are unable to cope with the real-time changes during the sonar search process, we establish a detection efficiency evaluation model of sonar search path by dynamic deduction based on Monte Carlo and discretization modeling methods. An efficiency evaluation method based on deep neural network (DNN) is proposed to meet the real-time and efficient requirements of task decision-making due to the time-consuming point by point deduction. The numerical results of case study show that using DNN can improve the time cost of the efficiency evaluation by more than 99%, shorten the time to sub-second, and the root mean square error of the prediction results are less than 0.04, and the determination coefficients are greater than 0.94, which effectively solve the problem of balancing the efficiency and robustness of sonar search path detection efficiency evaluation.

title: Robust-LaneNet: A Lightweight CNN Model for Autonomous Vehicle System in Adverse Visual Conditions
authors: Shivani Kumari, Pushpa Mamoria, Hemant Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Lane detection is still one of the major components for autonomous vehicle system (AVS), however, all current techniques suffer from significant degradation in adverse visual conditions. In this paper, we propose RobustLaneNet, a novel, lightweight CNN-based framework that incorporates weather robustness (WR) into its architecture. Robust-LaneNet utilizes depth-wise separable convolutions, a Weather-Robust Feature Enhancement Block, (WRFEB) and a hybrid loss function to obtain both pixel-level accuracy and structural continuity of lanes. Additionally, our weather-aware data augmentation strategy allows the model to generalize to difficult visual conditions such as rain, fog, and glare. In contrast to multi-stage or sensor fusion approaches, RobustLaneNet has an end-to-end architecture that does not require any additional processing overhead, allowing it to be efficiently deployed on embedded automotive systems. On CULane, Robust-LaneNet obtains 95.10% mAP@0.5 and 325 FPS, demonstrating competitive performance with many recently proposed lightweight lane detection approaches. Qualitatively, we demonstrate Robust-LaneNet's capability to maintain better lane continuity under low visibility conditions. Our results show that Robust-LaneNet not only offers accurate and reliable perception in poor visual conditions but also operates in real time, suggesting that it is a prime candidate for inclusion into future autonomous vehicle and driver-assistance systems.

title: ProJenius: The Project Management Chatbot
authors: Kanishka S, Suganya D, Padma Kumari P
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Project management is not an easy task as one has to balance project deadline, resources, and communication with all the involved stakeholders without overlooking the achievement of project objectives. The traditional instruments are inclined to use a manual and repetitive monitoring that reduces the efficiency and limits the breadth of the strategic planning. It is a work that seeks to research the ways in which project management can be enhanced through artificial intelligence in the form of conversational systems that will simplify the interaction process and provide decision support. The proposed solution is a chatbot called Projenius that is an AI-based app written in Python, Streamlit and SQLite. It allows users to search, update and visualize project data in natural language interaction. The system promotes the interactive and proactive control as it contains the functionality of four cooperation models: Joint Activity Theory, Shared Mental Model, Augmentation and Symbiotic. Time schedule tracking, balancing of tasks, and profit-cost ratio were included in order to address the common project management challenges, and dashboards and alerts offer the required insight and clarity to the team members. The outcome of the testing experiment proved that the chatbot had a retrieval precision of 95 %, an update efficiency of 92 %, and saved over 60 % of time when contrasted with manual operations. Such results highlight the fact that it can save time in redundant coordination activities, and more emphasis can be placed on high-order decision-making. Lastly, Projenius demonstrates that conversational AI has the potential of making project management more participatory, efficient, and user friendly in accordance with broader objectives of innovation, productivity, and sustainable digital infrastructure.

title: Survey of Disaggregated Memory: Cross-Layer Technique Insights for Next-Generation Datacenters
authors: Jing Wang, Chao Li, Taolei Wang, Jinyang Guo, Hanzhang Yang, Yiming Zhuansun, Minyi Guo
publish: ACM Computing Surveys
abstract: None

title: AI-Driven Dual-Mode Handover Optimization for 3D Mobile UAVs: Adaptive Threshold Control Meets CNN-Transformer Prediction
authors: Yimeng Shang, Tong Liu, Jian Ma, Nan Hu, Lei Xia, Wenzhi Li, Lei Zhang, Wenying Yang, Yitong Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: To address the challenges faced by low-altitude unmanned aerial vehicle(UAV) communications in cellular networks— namely weak horizontal side-lobe coverage and frequent handovers caused by traditional handover mechanisms—this paper proposes a dual-dimensional collaborative control approach that integrates adaptive handover optimization and time-series prediction. The goal is to enhance the stability and coverage quality of UAV communications during handovers. Firstly, a cascaded dynamic cell individual offset (CIO) adjustment model is constructed, along with a dynamic tuning mechanism for handover event triggering parameters, enabling intelligent adjustment of handover thresholds and effectively reducing the probability of invalid handovers. Secondly, a prediction model combining multi-scale convolutional kernel groups with self-attention mechanisms is developed to accurately extract temporal features of the signal and forecast future reference signal received power (RSRP) trends. This prediction provides valuable prior information for handover decision-making. Building upon these components, a collaborative decision-making workflow is established: the predictive outputs act as a forward-looking guide for the UAHO module, dynamically pre-adjusting the CIO parameters only for candidate cells with stable predicted RSRP trends, thereby proactively avoiding ping-pong effects and suboptimal handovers. Simulation results in dense urban scenarios show that, compared with the traditional A3 handover mechanism, the proposed method reduces the average number of handovers from 25.2 to 8.2, achieving a reduction of approximately 67.46%, while maintaining a high-quality coverage with an average RSRP of -73.215 dBm. Consequently, it offers a highly robust and efficient solution for reliable handovers in low-altitude UAV communications.

title: Wireless Charging and Data Relaying in WSNs Using an AAV-Mounted Active RIS
authors: Bo Wu, Xilong Liu, Nirwan Ansari
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: With the widespread deployment of wireless sensor networks (WSNs), energy replenishment and data collection of wireless sensor nodes (SNs) have emerged as vital research challenges. In existing studies, on one hand, autonomous aerial vehicles (AAVs) are employed to facilitate wireless charging for SNs within clusters, yet the fairness of energy replenishment among these SNs has been overlooked. On the other hand, as a novel and flexible data collection method, AAVs equipped with active reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) can reduce hardware requirements and signal processing complexity on the AAV side; however, the additional energy consumption introduced by active RIS operation is generally not considered. Therefore, in this work, we focus on utilizing AAV-mounted active RIS in conjunction with a non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) scheme to enable green (renewable) energy far-field wireless charging and data relaying for WSNs. We define the AAV’s service as powering SNs to reach their target energy thresholds and collecting the SNs’ data via the active RIS. We develop models for active RIS-aided data relaying and for determining the optimal reflection coefficients. We then formulate an optimization problem to maximize the number of SNs that can be served by the AAV. Given the NP-hard nature of the problem, we further propose a two-step solution with corresponding heuristic algorithms to efficiently solve it. Finally, extensive simulation results demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed solution.

title: 2026 Systems of Signals Generating and Processing in the Field of on Board Communications

title: Phygital Twin IoT: A Hardware-Software Decomposition Architecture for Scalable and Secure Digital Twin IoT Systems
authors: Ahcene Bounceur, Madani Bezoui, Foudil Mir, Adel Oulefki, Mohamed-Amine Ouamri, Huseyin Seker, Sebti Foufou, Abbes Amira, Yassine Himeur
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: This paper introduces Phygital Twin IoT (PT-IOT), a new and innovative architecture that combines a Digital Twin (DT) with a Physical Twin (PT) to create a flexible and scalable framework for modeling and managing real-world systems. The PT replicates the hardware components of the system, particularly the microcontroller, as a virtual counterpart that is programmed directly at the hardware level. This design ensures that the DT remains independent of the underlying hardware, enabling seamless adaptability to technological upgrades or security enhancements. The proposed architecture is based on the MQTT protocol to facilitate communication between physical and virtual components through an E-Program, which serves as a software coordinator. This approach supports real-time synchronization between physical and virtual nodes, making it highly suitable for large-scale applications such as smart cities, smart parking, and smart buildings. Notably, only the PT requires reprogramming when hardware changes occur, preserving the integrity of the DT. An additional feature of this framework is the ability to execute the E-Program externally on remote servers, allowing for continuous operation and advanced decision-making processes. This capability is particularly advantageous for critical applications, including emergency management and resource optimization. Analytical modeling of this architecture indicates an estimated 15-20% reduction in communication latency compared to direct microcontroller interfacing, while maintaining full functional equivalence. The proposed architecture was validated using the CupCarbon platform and its scalability and practicality make it a promising solution for modern IoT-based systems. Analytical results demonstrate superior performance in terms of scalability and energy efficiency compared to traditional DT architectures. Future research will focus on improving the framework with advanced decision-making algorithms and deploying it in real-world scenarios.

title: SynTrack: Multimodal Multi-Object Tracking for Autonomous Driving via Multi-Scale Cross-Modal Frequency Fusion and Temporal Contrastive Alignment
authors: Zhihao Su, Lei Yang, Mimi Zhao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Multimodal multi-object tracking has emerged as a crucial task in autonomous driving, aiming to perceive and track dynamic objects through the integration of visual, auditory, and linguistic cues. However, this task faces two major challenges: insufficient multimodal fusion, particularly the loss of frequency-domain information during spatial-only processing, and weak temporal semantic consistency, which often leads to tracking drift across frames. To address these issues, previous studies have explored spatial–frequency fusion networks and contrastive learning frameworks that align heterogeneous features across modalities. While these methods have achieved notable progress, they still suffer from limited adaptability to complex multimodal interactions and unstable long-term dependencies. To overcome these limitations, we propose SynTrack, a synergistic multimodal tracking framework that unifies frequency-domain fusion and contrastive temporal learning. SynTrack introduces two key components: a Multi-scale Cross-modal Frequency Fusion Module (MCFM) to enhance cross-modal representation through dual-domain attention, and a Multimodal Contrastive Tracking Learning (MCTL) strategy to enforce temporal coherence and semantic alignment across modalities. Extensive experiments on newly curated multimodal and text-guided benchmarks demonstrate that SynTrack achieves superior tracking accuracy, stability, and generalization under diverse environmental conditions, validating its effectiveness and scalability for real-world autonomous driving scenarios.

title: Configuration Optimization Strategy of Magnetic Soft Robot for Robot-Assisted Intervention_supp1-3680982.pdf
authors: Jinhui Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Magnetic soft robots (MSRs) are promising tools for precise intravascular interventions due to their flexibility and magnetic actuation capability, yet existing designs face fundamental trade-offs: magnetic continuum robots offer uniform deformation but suffer from weak magnetic responsiveness, while magnet-tip robots provide strong actuation but lack flexibility and workspace. Previous optimization strategies have largely treated geometry, material distribution, and magnetic configuration separately, leaving their coupled effects insufficiently understood. Here, we introduce a comprehensive theoretical model that unifies elastic, magnetic, gravitational, and external forces to analyze the MSR’s dynamic response and energy distribution. Guided by this model, we design a model-guided MSR with tailored magnetic distribution and structural configuration, achieving a normalized forward workspace of 0.28, which is nearly eight times larger than that of conventional continuum designs and more than twenty-seven times greater than typical magnet-tip robots, thereby establishing a new state of the art among 31 existing designs. Building upon this advance, we develop a Bio-Inspired Teleoperated Magnetic Navigation System (BITMNS) that integrates the model-guided MSR with a robotic magnetic manipulator and delivery device. In-vivo rabbit experiments demonstrate precise navigation through tortuous vascular pathways to the renal artery, while remote operation eliminates operator radiation exposure (238 mGy → 0) and force analyses confirm robustness under loads up to 9.5 times the safety threshold. Collectively, these advances expand the navigable vascular workspace, eliminate operator radiation exposure, and establish a safety margin nearly one order of magnitude above clinical thresholds, thereby demonstrating a concrete pathway toward safer and more effective endovascular interventions.

title: A 38–41 GHz 27-dBm Direct-Conversion I/Q Transmitter in 0.12- μ m GaN Technology With Enhanced Linearity for 4096-QAM Modulation
authors: Cheng-Yang Lee, Wei-Chih Chang, Po-Yuan Chen, Hong-Yeh Chang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a 38–41 GHz direct-conversion I/Q transmitter in 0.12-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m GaN technology that achieves high linearity and low error-vector magnitude (EVM) for high-order 4096-quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM). The proposed architecture integrates two high-swing active double-sideband (DSB) modulators, local oscillator (LO) amplifier, and a two-stage RF power amplifier (PA). To minimize amplitude modulation (AM)–AM and AM—phase modulation (PM) distortions across wide power range, the DSB modulators employ reflection-type modulation with distortion-minimization biasing, together with a differential amplifier and PA featuring conduction-angle optimization. These techniques collectively suppress second-order and third-order distortions and improve mixer-core symmetry. The demonstrated transmitter delivers output powers of 27, 25, and 22 dBm under quadrature phase shift keying (QPSK), 64-QAM, and 4096-QAM modulation schemes, respectively, corresponding to minimum EVM of 0.8%, 0.7%, and 1.4%. This work represents one of the first GaN zero-IF I/Q transmitters to simultaneously achieve high output power, wide bandwidth, and EVM performance suitable for emerging millimeter-wave high-order modulation transmitters.

title: AAF-Net: An Adaptive Attention Fusion Network for Landslide Change Detection
authors: Shengkang Li, Huiquan Wang, Peixing Cui
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Landslide change detection is critical for disaster monitoring but is plagued by two challenges that cause existing methods to fail: 1) missed detections when post-landslide areas (e.g., landslide soil) are spectrally similar to the pre-existing landscape (e.g., bare soil), as existing fusion strategies (e.g., differencing, concatenation) often face challenges in fully resolving extreme ambiguity; and 2) fragmented and incomplete segmentations, as existing decoders lack a mechanism to enforce global completeness while refining local boundaries. To solve these, we propose the Adaptive Attention Fusion Network (AAF-Net). Its uniqueness lies in two core innovations: 1) a Triplet Attention Fusion Module (TAFM), which models the full temporal triplet (pre-change, post-change, and difference) simultaneously, fundamentally resolving the ambiguity that other methods cannot. 2) a Hierarchical Attention-Guided Decoder (HAGD), whose unique coarse-to-fine strategy uses deep semantic guidance to establish global completeness, and then uses this as a spatial guide to refine precise local boundaries. On the GVLM-CD dataset, AAF-Net achieves a 92.11% F1-score and 85.38% IoU, significantly outperforming other state-of-the-art methods while being highly efficient (5.9 GFLOPs). This demonstrates a robust solution for post-disaster landslide detection and identification.

title: Wide-Angle Ultra-Wideband Tightly Coupled Array With Asymmetric Dipole Arms and Feeding Lines
authors: Runze Zhang, Wu Ren, Zhenghui Xue, Weiming Li, Haiyan Tian
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a dual-polarized tightly coupled dipole array based on an asymmetric architecture. Through co design of asymmetric feeding and radiating structures, the wide angle scanning bandwidth and port isolation of the array are effectively enhanced. Specifically, unequal-length dipole arms are adopted to suppress resonance modes that limit isolation. A balun composed of feeding lines with different diameters ensures stable impedance matching under wide-angle scanning. The array achieves a 4.5:1 bandwidth from 4 to 18 GHz, supporting ±60° scanning in both E- and H-planes with an active voltage standing wave ratio below 3. The port isolation remains better than 20 dB across the operating band, while the array height is only 0.09λ at 4 GHz. A 10×10 array prototype was fabricated and measured, showing good agreement with simulated results. This validates the integrated advantages of the design in achieving ultra-wide bandwidth, wide-angle scanning, and high isolation, making it well-suited for modern wireless communication systems.

title: AI-Driven Stacked Ensemble Intelligence for Robust Link Quality Classification and Adaptive Resource Management in Satellite Terrestrial Integrated Networks
authors: Rajesh Kumar Dhanaraj, M. Maragatharajan, Aanjankumar Sureshkumar, S. Poonkuntran, Dragan Pamucar
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The Satellite-Terrestrial Integrated Networks (STIN) were emerged as a key architectural pattern for attaining seamless, global and resilient wireless connectivity by adding extensive coverage of satellite systems with the high capacity and low latency of terrestrial networks. In spite of their advantages, STINs face significant challenges arising from heterogeneous link qualities, dynamic network topologies, long propagation delay and highly variable channel conditions which may complicate reliable and adaptable communication. The accurate and timely assessment of link quality is essential to enable effective resource management, adaptive modulation and coding and robust network control in space-ground integrated environments. In this study, AI-based link quality classification model for STINs based on a stacked ensemble learning architecture. This model combines multiple lightweight machine learning classifiers and a meta-level learner to capture complex non-linear relationships among satellite orbital parameters, spatial characteristics, and link dynamics. The framework categorizes satellite-terrestrial links into three operational states as Good, Moderate and Poor, which provides actionable intelligence for cross-layer resource allocation and adaptive communication strategies. The extensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed work attains 96.79% classification accuracy and 96.88% macro averaged F1-score where Decision tree as 85% and Machine Learning Based attain 88%. This indicates highly balanced and robust performance across all link classes. The confusion matrix analysis reveals that the misclassification occurs only between adjacent link quality states with no critical misclassifications between good and poor links. This ensures high reliability for operational decision-making. When compared to single-model baselines, the proposed approach increases prediction stability and robustness under heterogeneous and dynamic STIN conditions. The results confirmed the machine learning-assisted link quality intelligence can serve as a practical and efficient enabler for dynamic resource management in STIN. The model is computationally effective, scalable and readily deployable within next-generation STIN control planes which supports reliable communication for broadband access, emergency services and IoT applications.

title: Assessing Maintenance of Medium Voltage Cable Networks Under Time-Varying Loading
authors: Jochen Lorenz Cremer
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: The electrification and ongoing energy transition lead to systematic changes in electricity loading and variability in power systems. Distribution systems were designed for regular operating patterns, assuming constant low loading. Now, operators need to assess whether their assets can withstand more, as well as time-varying loading. Operating the system at or near its ampacity potentially accelerates thermal ageing, so the question arises: ‘how much can one operate at the limits while keeping maintenance and failures low?’ This paper introduces a novel approach that derives a time-varying Weibull approximation of failure rates using thermal models and provides a shortcut method to quantify maintenance implications under time-varying loading for heterogeneous MV cable populations. The case studies investigate a dataset from Denmark and the Oberrhein Medium Voltage (MV) system in Germany, studying ageing assets and the interplay with loading, and replacement paradigms of two different cable insulation types. The studies demonstrate that a small fraction of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$25\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of old, low-quality cables leads to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$82\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of failures, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.4\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of the time of highest loading can cause <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$46\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of cable ageing. The case studies also demonstrate that maintenance needs may be between <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10-300$</tex-math></inline-formula> times higher under future loading conditions associated with the energy transition, specifically in networks that have older PILC cables. This paper provides a new tool for operators to plan maintenance under more realistic, future operating conditions.

title: Hybrid Orchestration of AI Services and Microservices in Multi-Edge Collaboration Based on Lagrangian Relaxation Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Jiaxiang Xu, Zhaoyi Wang, Di Han, Yi Hu, Fuming Fan, Menglan Hu, Kai Peng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The rapid development of artificial intelligence has promoted the application of AI services in various fields. The combination of the microservice architecture and the “AI as a Service” (AaaS) to form an intelligent microservice system can realize the orchestration of intelligent applications. In intelligent applications, AI services often require the key functions of accompanying microservices, such as service discovery, data collection, gateway routing, etc., to ensure the stability and efficiency. Otherwise, it will lead to the incompleteness of applications, severely affecting the overall performance of the system. Therefore, fine-grained hybrid orchestration of AI services and microservices is crucial. Nevertheless, it faces major challenges in the resource-constrained edge environment: complex dependencies, frequent data communication, and heterogeneous resource competition between microservices and AI services. Moreover, the tight coupling between deployment and routing leads to complex joint optimization problems. Considering either deployment or routing separately will lead to poor system performance. However, most of the existing studies ignore the above problems.To this end, this paper investigates the hybrid orchestration problem. First, we model intelligent applications as hybrid service invocation chains. Then, based on Jackson queuing networks and multi-instance models, we deeply study the complex dependencies among hybrid services and the heterogeneous resource allocation problem, and conduct an accurate analysis of the request delay and system energy consumption. Furthermore, we innovatively propose Lagrangian Relaxed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (LR_DDPG), which introduces a cost mechanism and transforms the constrained hybrid orchestration process into an unconstrained optimization problem by Lagrange relaxation to find the optimal deployment and routing strategy under complex constraints. Finally, the experimental results show that our algorithm achieves significant advantages in terms of latency, energy consumption, and request success rate.

title: Divo: Learning a Stable and Effective Query Optimizer with a Diverse Workload
authors: Tianyi Chen, Jun Gao, Yaofeng Tu, Yang Lin, Mo Xu, Yinjun Han

title: TIG-IM: a Two-Stage Inductive GNN with Multi-Task Learning for Influential Node Identification in Complex Networks
authors: Yonggang Li, Zhili Xiao, Yuanjin Zhang, Longjiang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Identifying influential nodes in complex networks poses a significant challenge, requiring a delicate balance among efficiency, accuracy, and redundancy elimination. To address this, we propose TIG-IM (A Two-stage Inductive GNN for Influence Maximization), a novel framework based on GNN. In Stage 1, we construct a multi-task learning-based global scorer. This scorer utilizes a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to simultaneously regress the SIR propagation score and our newly proposed 3D-Bridging Centrality. This multi-task design enables efficient identification of cross-community bridge nodes to generate a high-quality candidate pool. In Stage 2, we design a local selector that introduces a composite loss function with sparsity and diversity regularization. This selector performs a secondary re ranking on the candidate subgraph to produce a final seed set that is both structurally dispersed and possesses complementary propagation potential. Theoretical analysis proves that our scorer satisfies monotonicity and that the local loss can be interpreted as a MAP estimation. Extensive experiments on twelve real-world networks from diverse domains, including social, academic, and biological, demonstrate that TIG-IM significantly outperforms various state-of-the-art baselines in both spreading effectiveness and computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the synergistic value of our three core components: 3D-Bridging Centrality, sparsity penalty, and diversity-based re-ranking.
