title: A lock-in amplifier (LIA)-based Mach-Zehnder mod-ulator bias control system
authors: Jiahong Zhang, Hong Chen, Yingna Li, Xin Zhang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: In this paper, a lock-in amplifier (LIA)-based bias con trol system for the Mach-Zehnder modulator (MZM) is designed and experimentally verified. The LIA is employed to extract the harmonics of the pilot signal, forming a narrow band-pass filter that effectively reduces the amplitude of the pilot signal, thus improving the third-order spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR3). Experimental results show that the MZM bias point can be stabilized within ±0.76° at room temperature for 12 hours and can be stabilized within ±0.83° in 2 hours when the temperature ranges from 20°C to 50°C. Besides, for the pilot signal amplitude VPT = 30 mV, the SFDR3 is 65.47 dB, which is improved by 8.34 dB compared that with VPT = 50 mV.

title: Range-Outage Probability for Near-Field s-FAMA Random Access with Short Packets
authors: Fusheng Zhao
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This letter investigates near-field slow fluid antenna multiple access (s-FAMA) for joint decoding and ranging. Since conventional outage metrics fail to capture sensing precision, we propose the per-user range-outage probability (PUROP). PUROP quantifies the probability that range estimation error exceeds a threshold, explicitly conditioned on finite-blocklength decoding success. We derive a closed-form near-field Cramér–Rao lower bound (CRLB) using an effective selection-gain model and map it to PUROP via a Gaussian approximation. Numerical results demonstrate that s-FAMA comprehensively enhances sensing and communication compared to fixed-antenna systems.

title: Deep Information-Balanced Multimodal Learning
authors: Yang Qin, Yanglin Feng, Yuan Sun, Dezhong Peng, Xi Peng, Peng Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multimodal learning aims to integrate diverse data sources to capture more comprehensive information about things, thus enhancing perception and understanding of the real world. However, inherent discrepancies between different modalities often lead to imbalanced optimization during multimodal learning, hindering performance improvement. To address this issue, in this paper, we present a Multimodal Information Balance (MIB) theory, grounded in Information Theory, to reveal that this imbalance arises from the imbalanced retention of complementary information during modality fusion, providing an intuitive and explainable perspective on the issue. Building on this insight, we propose a theoretical MIB criterion to adaptively balance the preservation of complementary information across individual modalities, thereby facilitating multimodal fusion. Using this criterion, we develop an Information-Balanced Multimodal Learning (IBML) framework to mine comprehensive and balanced multimodal information, achieving optimal learning. More specifically, IBML introduces Balance Information Optimization (BIO) module to maximize tractable lower bound objectives derived from the MIB criterion according to the optimization discrepancies across modalities, ensuring balanced retention of complementary information and enhancing information contributions during multimodal fusion. In addition, we present a supplementary and provable Task Complexity Modulation (TCM) module based on the MIB criterion to adjust task complexity discrepancies across input modalities, thus indirectly promoting the balanced preservation of complementary information throughout the learning process. Extensive experiments are conducted on eight multimodal datasets, spanning audio-visual recognition, image-text classification, and 2D-3D recognition, to verify the superiority and effectiveness of IBML. The code will be released publicly after in-peer review.

title: A High-Rate-Compatible Algebraic GC-LDPC Code for 3D TLC NAND Flash Memory
authors: Linxin Yin, Xiongfei Zhai, Yi Fang, Qi’ao Zhu, Guojun Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: The escalating storage density of three-dimensional (3D) NAND flash memory introduces heightened channel noise, significantly degrading channel quality and exacerbating the raw bit error rate (RBER). The fixed and unchangeable code rate of the traditional global coupled low-density-parity-check (GC-LDPC) codes fails to address the performance mismatch arising from the stochastic temporal and spatial fluctuations in 3D NAND flash channels. To address the above issue efficiently, in this paper we investigate high-rate-compatible LDPC codes in 3D NAND flash memory. Firstly, we analyze the flash memory channel characteristics from the FPGA test platform and model the 3D triple-level cell (TLC) NAND flash channel. Subsequently, we propose a novel High-Rate-Compatible Algebraic GC-LDPC (HRC-A-GC-LDPC) code and conduct a comprehensive theoretical analysis of its structure and performance. Specifically, the HRC-A-GC-LDPC code features an information bit length of 4 KB and supports dynamic adjustment to multiple code rates, enabling it to match the varying characteristics of 3D NAND flash channels. This paper also examines and analyzes various construction methods for the HRC-A-GC-LDPC code corresponding to different column weights (CWs), providing insights into its design flexibility. Simulation results demonstrate that, in 3D TLC NAND flash memory, the proposed HRC-A-GC-LDPC code outperforms conventional GC-LDPC codes in both error correction capability and extended memory lifetime, validating its suitability for high-density 3D NAND flash applications.

title: Safe and Interactive Integration of Prediction and Planning for Autonomous Vehicles with Residual Chain Loss
authors: Donglei Rong, Congcong Bai, Wenbin Yao, Qiaoqiao Ren, Sheng Jin, Min Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The Integration of Prediction and Planning (IPP) methodology enhances interrelationships between autonomous vehicle modules while improving system interpretability. However, safety, interaction, and performance evaluation present significant challenges that may lead to safety risks. This paper introduces IPP* (Safe and Interactive Integration of Prediction and Planning with Residual Chain Loss), a framework that addresses these challenges through three key components. First, a safety judgment module employs rule-based methods to assess prediction trustworthiness and ensure stability before planning. Second, a Nash Equilibrium-based approach facilitates iterative refinement between prediction and planning modules, creating a game-theoretic framework that enables mutual learning and system optimization. Third, a Residual Chain Loss Function addresses covariate bias in imitation learning by analyzing disparities between predicted and planned trajectories. The results indicate that IPP* achieves an Average Displacement Error (ADE) of 0.74 m and a Final Displacement Error (FDE) of 0.89 m, and delivers an average L2 distance (L2) of 0.44 m with an average Collision Rate (CR) of 0.27%. Ablation results further verify the complementary contributions of the safety judgment module, the game-based interaction module, and the residual chain loss function, while sensitivity analyses over key thresholds and loss weights indicate stable behavior and clarify safety–performance trade-offs.

title: Scattering-Aware Graph Neural Network with Transfer Learning for Few-shot Aircraft Detection in SAR Images
authors: Qian Guo, Jiyun Chen, Jingjing Zhang, Hui Bi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep neural networks integrating explainable information have shown significant progress in synthetic aperture radar-automatic target recognition (SAR-ATR). Nevertheless, their underlying mechanisms and performance in few-shot object detection (FSOD) scenarios remain insufficiently explored. From a disentanglement perspective, FSOD can be decomposed into a detection task followed by a classification task. The former emphasizes discriminative features between targets and background for localization, while the latter focuses on intra-class distinctive attributes for fine-grained classification. Therefore, this paper proposes a scattering-aware graph neural network (SAGN) with transfer learning for aircraft detection in SAR imagery. The framework explicitly leverages scattering-aware prior knowledge tailored for the two subtasks, comprising two core components: a salient feature generator (SFG) for localization and a structure feature injector (SFI) for classification. First, we analyze the effectiveness of applying disentangled prior knowledge in FSOD tasks. Subsequently, the SFG module is introduced for localization, consisting of a salient feature extraction module (SEM) that enhances distinctive target-background features, and a context-aware module (CAM) that captures global spatial dependencies through expanded receptive fields. For fine-grained classification, the SFI module incorporates structural and semantic target features to enable efficient representation learning under limited samples. Especially, a triplet loss based on anchor, positive, and negative topological features is designed to improve generalization in a self-supervised manner. Furthermore, a transfer learning strategy leveraging large-scale base-class data is devised to enhance few-shot adaptation. Comprehensive experiments on the SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset demonstrate that SAGN exhibits outstanding performance, with 0.507 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${mAP}\_\text{novel}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 0.808 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${mAP}\_\text{base}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and 0.722 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${mAP}\_\text{all}$</tex-math></inline-formula> under 30-shot settings, validating its effectiveness and superiority in FSOD tasks.

title: From Prefix Cache to Fusion RAG Cache: Accelerating LLM Inference in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Jiahao Wang, Weiyu Xie, Mingxing Zhang, Boxin Zhang, Jianwei Dong, Yuening Zhu, Chen Lin, Jingqi Tang, Yaochen Han, Zhiyuan Ai, Xianglin Chen, Yongwei Wu, Congfeng Jiang

title: Multi-Channel Signal Integration for Weak Target Detection in Multistatic Passive Radar
authors: Ziqian Huang, Jun Wang, Zuhan Cheng, Menglei Li, Luo Zuo
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In this paper, an innovative multi-channel signal integration method for multistatic passive radar systems (MPRs) is proposed, aimed at fully leveraging spatial diversity gain to enhance the detection capability of weak targets. Multi-channel signal integration in MPRs faces several challenges: target motion-induced range migration (RM) and Doppler spreading (DS) within channels, bistatic geometries lead to inter-channel peak position discrepancies, and phase inconsistency caused by target scattering characteristics under varying observation perspectives further complicates signal alignment. To address these challenges, a target detector based on a maximum likelihood framework is constructed, which systematically integrates range-Doppler migration compensation, inter-channel geometric discrepancy correction, and phase inconsistency modeling. In response to the nonlinearity and high computational complexity of the detector, a low-complexity solution is further proposed in this paper. First, the received signal is processed in frames, with the framing criterion ensuring that no range-unit or Doppler-unit shifts occur within a single frame. Then, by transforming the high-dimensional parameter optimization problem into the computation of a low-dimensional likelihood domain test matrix. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through multidimensional simulations, including energy focusing characteristics, factors influencing detection performance, and comparative algorithm analysis. The results demonstrate that this method can achieve efficient multi-channel signal accumulation with low computational overhead, significantly improving target detection performance.

publish: IEEE MultiMedia

title: Improving IoT Data Security with Blockchain and SHA-3-based Integrity Validation for Real-Time Transmission
authors: Dhamodharan R, Rajeswari S V, A.Ananthi Christy, Charanya P, Guma Ali, Thella Preethi Priyanka
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The increased exponentially use of Internet of Things (IoT) tools has given serious considerations to the issue of security and integrity of data transferred on real time networks or questions of authenticity of data. This study suggests a secure messaging architecture of combining Blockchains with integrity validation protocol of SHA-3 assuring non-manipulated IoT real-time data streaming. The architecture proposed here would leverage the Keccak based SHA3-256 cryptographic hash algorithm to establish a unique data fingerprint to each transmission that it would then store on a Blockchain ledger through Proof-of-Authority (PoA) consensus mechanism. This enables receivers to match the hashes to validate authenticity of data received on the one hand, and compute a new hash to check the one attached by the sender on the other hand. It was then constructed with Python 3.13 and the libraries used included: hashlib, pandas, matplotlib, and time and simulations were carried out on synthetically produced sensor values representing temperature and humidity measurements of 10 abstract IoT devices. The data packets contain nonce, timestamp in real-time, and unique device ID to increase the entropy. The process of integrity validation and blockchain logging was tested with pace of hash computing, the delay of blockchain logging and the authenticity of verification. It was experimentally observed that 100 percent data integrity was achieved by the system in normal circumstances where hashing time of the system was on the order of 0.11 ms, blockchain logging latency ranged between 50 to 150 ms, and verification was as observed < 0.06 ms. These findings confirm the viability of the combination between blockchain and SHA-3 in lightweight, secure, and real-time communication of IoT. There is a guarantee of reproducibility and scalability to be deployed in real-world systems (home automation in smart homes, industry automation, and patient monitoring).

title: An Improved ConvNeXt Model for Insect Image Classification and Identification*
authors: Yixiao Wang, Xin Chen, Zuorui Shen
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Accurate classification and identification of insect species is fundamental for agricultural and forestry pest monitoring and biodiversity conservation. To address the challenge of effectively modeling fine-grained features in insect images against complex backgrounds, this paper proposes an improved insect image classification model, Improve_ConvNeXt, based on the ConvNeXt architecture. Firstly, a dataset named IP20 containing 20 categories with a total of 14,801 high-quality insect images is constructed by screening the IP102 dataset, and supervised data augmentation is employed to alleviate class imbalance. Subsequently, 11 mainstream convolutional neural network (CNN) models are systematically compared under unified experimental settings, with results indicating that ConvNeXt demonstrates optimal performance across multiple metrics. Building upon this, the feature modeling capability of ConvNeXt is specifically enhanced by introducing the Hybrid Attention module (HAT) and the Spatial and Channel reconstruction Convolution module (SCConv). Experimental results show that Improve_ConvNeXt achieves a classification accuracy of 96.4% on the test set, outperforming the original ConvNeXt model in metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score. The findings suggest that the proposed method can enhance insect image classification performance while maintaining high computational efficiency, offering an effective solution for intelligent insect monitoring.

title: Real-Valued Arithmetic Linearly-Constrained Adaptive Arrays for GNSS Applications
authors: Victor I. Djigan
publish: 2026 Systems of Signals Generating and Processing in the Field of on Board Communications
abstract: Adaptive Antenna Array (AAA) is an efficient tool to combat interferences that shares frequency band with informational signals. A modern application of the technology is the AAA usage as the antennas of the ground-based receivers in the Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs). The most efficient adaptive algorithms for the AAA weights calculation are the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) ones, where efficiency means transient response duration and steady-state minimized cost function value. However, the computational complexity (in terms of arithmetic operations number per iteration) of the RLS algorithms is big enough. At the same time, this complexity can be reduced about two times in the AAAs with odd symmetry as it is shown in this paper. This is achieved due to the ability to execute almost all calculations of the adaptive algorithms in real-valued arithmetic in such AAAs. This paper presents the GNSS AAA architecture, the computational procedures of the linearly constrained versions of adaptive RLS algorithms (matrix inversion lemma and square root QR decomposition based), and the simulation results that validate these algorithms and confirm the considered AAA efficiency.

title: QuantumPay: A Multichain Blockchain Payment System using Quantum Cryptography and AI Driven Fraud Detection
authors: Rabees Paroshan, Srivaitheeswari.M, Abhishek Kumar.S.A, S. Pavithra
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Quantum computing has positioned itself as a serious threat to traditional cryptography, undermining the very foundation of present-day methods of transaction and the popularly used digital payment systems. Here lies an interest in proposing a platform that can remedy these quantum-age risks present in payment mechanisms. The intended aim thus becomes that of building a secure and scalable payment system that uses quantum computing algorithms to train AI models for fraud detection in real-time, QKD to manage key security, and PQC to securely encrypt transaction data. For privacy, ZKP will be used to verify the transaction without revealing any details from it. Also, the platform will integrate multichain blockchains with quantum sharding, allowing separate processing, and distribution of transaction storage. Experimental results have shown that the proposed platform can resist quantum attacks, attain more accuracy in ratio detection, and improve the transaction speed more than the average blockchain solutions. The novelty of this research stands on the enhanced multichain blockchain architecture and improved Zero-Knowledge Proof protocols, which improve scalability and privacy. By bringing multichain architecture, quantum computing, QKD, and PQC into one model, this platform sets the benchmark for a secure, scalable, and affordable digital payment system.

title: FPGA Implementation and Performance Analysis of 64-Bit Multiplier Architectures
authors: Thivagar Thiyagarajan, Vaishnavi P, Bhaskar Manickam
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Power-efficient high-frequency multipliers make a vital contribution to Digital Signal Processing (DSP) systems, embedded systems, Very Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) systems, and other high-performance computing systems. In this paper, optimised design and performance evaluation of different 64-bit multiplier architectures, including Array, Booth, Dadda, Vedic, and Wallace Tree (WT) multipliers, are explored. The designs are implemented in the Verilog hardware description language using the Xilinx Vivado 2022.2 platform and synthesized on the Kintex Ultrascale KCU105 evaluation board, aiming to optimize the Area, Power, and Delay (APD) parameters to enhance the overall system efficiency and computational performance. The Array multiplier (conventional multipliers), owing to its complex design, utilizes a higher amount of power and area. The Booth multiplier performs signed operations with reasonable efficiency. The Dadda multiplier reduces propagation delay through optimized reduction stages but increases power usage. The WT multiplier attains faster computation (carry-save addition) with balanced resource utilization. Among these architectures, the Vedic multiplier exhibits low power consumption and minimal resource utilization, making it the most efficient architecture for low-power, high-speed FPGA applications.

title: Lexi - A Local, Learnable Voice Assistant for System and Web
authors: B. Lavanya, Narra Tharun, N. Harsha Vardhan, B. Jaya Harsha Vardhan, M. Rajesh
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: While voice assistants have become pervasive across mobile devices and smart speakers, their underlying operations often depend on major technology companies, extensive cloud infrastructure, and substantial data collection. This paper introduces Lexi (Learning Expert Interface for System and Web Support), a unique voice assistant designed with a distinct operational philosophy. Lexi is a lightweight, Python-exclusive application developed to run directly on a user's local computer, specifically without requiring a graphical user interface. It utilizes spoken input to control computer functions, retrieve online information, and automate diverse tasks. Lexi is capable of understanding verbal instructions, providing audible responses, automating web-based activities, managing desktop features, accessing information from Wikipedia, processing PDF documents, generating QR codes, scheduling WhatsApp messages, maintaining contact lists, capturing screenshots, and reporting system diagnostics. The system's architecture emphasizes simplicity and clarity, ensuring its functionality is transparent for scrutiny and modification. This paper details Lexi's development, the Python libraries utilized, its supported command set, and crucial considerations regarding safety and privacy. A comprehensive analysis of its performance is also presented, covering aspects such as response speed, resilience in various noise conditions, and computational resource demands. Lexi demonstrates the viability of creating a robust voice assistant independently of complex cloud services, offering a valuable platform for education, experimentation, and enhancing personal productivity. (Note: "ASR" means automatic speech recognition in the figures and text.)

title: Leveraging Synthetic Multilingual Speech for Automatic Speech Recognition Adaptation: A Comprehensive Cross-Language Analysis
authors: Nishanth Prakash, Amruta Desai
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Contemporary automatic speech recognition (ASR) architectures necessitate extensive paired audio-text corpora for optimal convergence, yet acquisition of such datasets remains costly and time-consuming, particularly for multilingual deployment scenarios. This investigation presents a rigorous empirical analysis of synthetic speech generation leveraging XTTS for cross-lingual ASR domain adaptation across English (EN), Spanish (ES), and French (FR). We systematically decompose the efficacy trade-offs between synthetic and authentic data modalities, conducting controlled ablation studies on lexical and acoustic variability contributions to recognition performance. Our experimental methodology incorporates statistically robust quality validation pipelines and stratified data mixing protocols. Empirical results reveal distinct language-dependent scaling laws: French exhibits a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12 \times$</tex> synthetic-to-real data equivalence ratio for performance parity, Spanish demonstrates <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9 \times$</tex> scaling, while English requires <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times$</tex> magnification. We establish that lexical diversity dominates performance optimization in low-resource regimes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(500 ~\mathrm{h})$</tex>, whereas acoustic variability becomes the limiting factor beyond 1000h across all linguistic modalities. These findings establish quantitative guidelines for multilingual ASR optimization and underscore the critical importance of language-specific morphophonological considerations in synthetic data generation protocols.

title: Engineering System for Silicon Nanowire MEMS Accelerometers Based on Embedded Processing
authors: Zhiyuan Yin, Yi Wang, Qianlong Gao, Zhichao Lin, Kaitong Liao
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Silicon nanowires, with their outstanding piezoresistive effect and structural size advantages, serve as an ideal material for high-performance MEMS accelerometers. This study designed and implemented a MEMS accelerometer integrated with an embedded processing system based on silicon nanowires. The nanowire sensing element was fabricated using the VLS method, combined with SOI process technology to fabricate the cantilever beam structure, and a real-time processing platform based on FreeRTOS was developed. Through optimized analog front-end design and digital signal processing algorithms, the system achieves high-sensitivity, low-noise, and wide-bandwidth acceleration measurement performance, providing a novel solution for industrial vibration monitoring and structural health monitoring.

title: Multi-Modal Deep Learning Framework for Endometrial Cancer Classification
authors: Snitha Shetty, Sharon C Dsouza, Dhanya V Shetty, Harshitha, R Raksha, Sthuthi Dolpady
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Endometrial cancer classification plays a vital role in determining the right treatment and predicting the proper patient outcomes. Earlier, the diagnosis depended on either histopathological examination or clinical data alone, which can limit accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-modal deep learning model that combines histopathological images and clinical parameters to automatically classify endometrial cancer subtypes. Using the TCGA-UCEC dataset, our model integrates a convolutional neural network for image analysis with a dense neural network for clinical data, creating a unified framework that enhances diagnostic precision and supports better clinical decision making. Our model addresses the challenge of the same patient validation by considering both histopathological images and clinical data originating from identical patients. Trained on 15, 850 histopathology patches from TCGA-UCEC patients, the system achieved 89.5% training accuracy with 89.0% validation accuracy, demonstrating minimal overfitting and practical clinical utility for endometrial cancer diagnosis

title: Sentiment Analysis of Chinese Weibo Based on BERT-BiLSTM-CNN
authors: Chu Hong Yun, Hong Rong Rong
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: With the rapid development of social media, Weibo has become an important platform for expressing public emotions and opinions. Sentiment analysis, as a key task in natural language processing, is of great significance for understanding users’ sentiment orientations and monitoring public opinions. In order to more effectively capture the semantic and emotional information in Weibo texts, a Chinese Weibo sentiment analysis model based on BERT-BiLSTM-CNN is proposed. This model utilizes the BERT pre-trained language model to obtain the contextual semantic representation of the text, captures the sequential features of the text through BiLSTM, and combines CNN to extract local features, ultimately achieving sentiment classification. The experimental results show that this model achieves good performance in the task of Chinese Weibo sentiment analysis, with higher accuracy and F1 scores compared to other deep learning models.

title: 3D Printing Defect Detection Model Based on Improved YOLOV10n
authors: Shuxin Yang, Yu Deng, Weidong Huang, Yang Cheng
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Aiming at the defect detection of common multi-scale defects such as Spaghetti, Stringing, and Zits in Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printing, this study proposes an enhanced detector, GCFGA-YOLO, by integrating multiple feature-optimization strategies into YOLOv10. Specifically, Global Context Convolution (GCConv) replaces downsampling convolution to strengthen feature representations; directional and channel attention are introduced in a redesigned C2f-Cfb block to reinforce global features and enlarge the effective receptive field; a cross-gated attention-based CGAFusion module is embedded in the P3, P4, and P5 feature layers to adaptively fuse semantic and fine-grained information. Experiments on the same 3D printing defect dataset demonstrate that, compared with the YOLOv10 baseline and other mainstream defect detectors, the proposed model achieves a favorable balance between detection accuracy and real-time performance, enabling real-time defect monitoring in 3D printing processes.

title: Discovering the most severe k-point failure based on Reinforcement Learning
authors: Kejun Chen, Abhijeet Sahu, Truc Nguyen, Malik Hassanaly
publish: 2026 IEEE Green Technologies Conference (GreenTech)
abstract: Smart devices are essential to ensure the stability of the power grid and resilience to intermittent energy production. However, smart devices can also be the target of cyber adversaries that may exploit false data injection attacks (FDIAs) to induce unstable grid conditions. A practical consideration of FDIA mitigation approaches is addressed here: given a finite available budget, for which smart device should cyber-threat mitigation be deployed first? In this work, this question is answered by identifying the so-called most-sensitive devices, i.e., the devices that, if compromised, can let an adversary induce the most serious grid instabilities. The method proposed utilizes an adversarial reinforcement learning (RL) framework to identify the k-most-sensitive smart devices (here, smart inverters). The adversarial agent can tamper with the compromised inverters’ active and reactive operating power setup points, with the goal of maximizing voltage deviations. Numerical results show that the proposed RL method finds the optimal attack scenarios for 1-point failure and the near-optimal solution for the 2-point case. Additionally, the proposed RL method achieves an 8.8× speed-up ratio in running time compared to the brute force method for the 2-point case.

title: OPT-FPID: An Optimally Tuned Fuzzy PID Framework for High-Performance Visual Tracking Control Systems
authors: Chuang Yao, Fan Wang, Wen Zhang, Yingwei Xia
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: This letter proposes the Optimally Tuned Fuzzy PID (OPT-FPID) framework, a novel two-stage paradigm designed to address the complexity and inefficiency of high-dimensional parameter tuning for visual tracking sensor systems. In the offline stage, a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm, guided by a multi-objective cost function, systematically determines the optimal parameters for a hierarchical dual-mode fuzzy PID controller within a data-driven simulation. In the online stage, these pre-optimized parameters are deployed to the physical platform, enabling the hierarchical controller to achieve high-precision and robust real-time tracking. Experimental validation, including a comprehensive ablation study, quantitatively demonstrates that the OPT-FPID-tuned controller significantly outperforms manually tuned baselines in key dynamic performance metrics.

title: Design of a Direct-Estimation Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter-Based Digital Lock-in Amplifier
authors: Ziao Zhang, Ze Liu, Zewen Wan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Conventional digital lock-in amplifiers inherently struggle to balance high accuracy with fast response speed. To address this trade-off, this paper proposes a direct-estimation Innovation-Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (IAEKF) algorithm. This method replaces traditional low-pass filtering and demodulation stages by introducing an innovation-adaptive mechanism with orthogonal measurement noise decomposition to balance implementability and robustness. The study derives steady-state closed-loop error dynamics, mapping them to a standard second-order form to clarify the mechanism of spontaneous equivalent-bandwidth adjustment. MATLAB simulations and experiments show that the proposed IAEKF reduces the MAE by 4.4% and shortens the rise time by 22.6% compared with the conventional LPF baseline. The method remains robust under time-varying and nonstationary noise, making it suitable for precision weak-signal measurements.

title: RZF-AOR Precoding Optimization Algorithm for Massive MIMO Systems
authors: Zhe Liu, Junqin Wu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: Traditional precoding algorithms (MRT, ZF, MMSE, SLNR) involve excessively high computational loads in massive MIMO systems, and the resulting precoding delay can no longer meet the requirements of wireless communication.In massive MIMO systems. To solve the complexity problems of traditional precoding algorithms, we propose the Accelerated Over-Relaxation (AOR) method to replace the high-complexity matrix inversion operation in the original Regularized Zero-Forcing (RZF) precoding algorithm, resulting in an improved algorithm called RZF-AOR. The optimal correlation parameters for this algorithm are determined using the Nelder-Mead optimization method. Experimental simulations show that the proposed RZF-AOR precoding algorithm effectively reduces complexity by an order of magnitude compared to RZF precoding, achieves bit error rate performance close to that of RZF precoding with a very small number of iterations, and outperforms the bit error rate performance of Neumann series-based precoding algorithms

title: Improvement in Automated Diagnosis of Soft Tissues Tumors Using Deep Learning
authors: Russia S, Lanisa Bharathi K, Maheen Mohamed Ismayeel M, Sri Guru Visnu K, Jabeen Begum S, Dinesh Kumar V
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: We suggest in this paper a novel method that leverages the power of the neural network, specifically the ResNet50 model, to automatically and accurately classify Soft Tissue Tumors (STTs) with application mainly to skin tumors. Due to their diverse nature, STTs pose a problem of diagnosis, which might result in misdiagnosis, hence the urgent need of automated and precise diagnostic methods. The proposed method includes advanced data preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification techniques with a view to obtain a better classification performance and lower errors. The model will be evaluated on the multi-parametric Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) data, with a particular focus on the accurate tumor delineation, which is crucial for successful radiation therapy planning. The primary objective is to distinguish melanoma from normal skin with very high accuracy thus enhancing diagnostic accuracy, treatment planning and patient outcomes.

title: Feeding the GPUs: Node-Level Energy Modeling and Workload-Aware Efficiency in AI Inference Servers
authors: Prasanna Chandran Melnatami Krishnaram
publish: 2026 IEEE Green Technologies Conference (GreenTech)
abstract: A comparative energy consumption analysis of AMD EPYC vs. Intel Xeon head node processors in an 8×GPU AI inference server is presented. A detailed node level energy model is developed, covering the head node’s CPU, memory, I/O, storage, and cooling power, as well as the GPUs’ compute, memory, and interconnect power. Using empirical measurements on a system with 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs, the two CPU platforms are compared in terms of power draw and efficiency on representative inference workloads. Both configurations reach a similar peak power (~8–9 kW with 8 GPUs fully loaded), dominated by the GPUs. The AMD EPYC head node (higher core count, greater memory bandwidth) feeds the GPUs slightly better, achieving up to ~10% higher throughput in a multi-GPU inference benchmark than an Intel-based node at similar power. The Intel Xeon head node (fewer cores but higher per-core speed) offers lower I/O latency and a well-optimized software stack, benefiting certain latency-sensitive tasks.Energy efficiency depends on workload: if GPUs are saturated (e.g. large batches or many concurrent streams), the head node contributes <15% of total system power (only a few hundred watts), making GPU efficiency the primary factor. In more CPU-intensive or I/O heavy inference workloads (e.g. a recommender with CPU hosted data), AMD’s greater core count and memory throughput improve performance-per-watt, whereas under light loads Intel’s platform idles at a slightly lower baseline power. Key energy saving strategies are summarized. For example, maximizing hardware utilization via batching and parallelism which in one case improved throughput-per-watt by an order of magnitude and leveraging dynamic power management (CPU DVFS, GPU power capping, efficient cooling, etc.). These insights provide guidance for data center practitioners to maximize GPU utilization and overall energy efficiency in multi-GPU AI inference servers.

title: AI-Driven Smart Lighting with Smart Controls for Energy Conservation and Sustainable Agricultural Greenhouse Management
authors: Dr.S.Jayakumar, Dr.R.Meenakshi, Cheepurupalli Raghuram, Santosh Kumar Sahoo, Ayalapogu Ratna Raju, Sathishkumar V E
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The increasing need for energy-efficient solutions in agriculture has resulted in the incorporation of AI-driven smart lighting systems in greenhouse management. This work presents a smart lighting system that employs Q-learning, a reinforcement learning method, to enhance energy efficiency while promoting sustainable plant growth in greenhouses. The system modulates illumination intensity according to real-time variables such as plant species, growth phase, time of day, and external environmental conditions. Through continuous interaction with the greenhouse environment, the Q-learning model acquires the ability to make informed decisions that harmonize energy saving with ideal light conditions for plant health. Q-learning, an off-policy technique, enables the system to independently adjust and enhance its lighting schemes by obtaining feedback through incentives for efficient energy use or penalties for inadequate performance. Gradually, the system acquires knowledge about the most efficient measures to reduce energy expenses while maintaining factory production. The proposed method enables dynamic adjustment of greenhouse illumination, resulting in energy efficiency and sustainable agricultural results. This method not only diminishes operating expenses but also promotes sustainable agricultural practices, making it a viable solution for the future of precision farming.

title: Bridgecare: A Smart Connect System for The Homeless
authors: Amutha M, Gayathri C, Janaki J, Kaarunya A S, Saratha M
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The issue of homelessness continues to rank high as one of the most important social issues in the world, ever increasing is the gap between the homeless and the being of people with basic needs such as food, shelter, health care, and education. In spite of constant efforts made by and alongside NGOs, benefactors, volunteers, and the government, these problems remain unresolved mainly due to poor planning, lack of transparency, and improper allocation of resources. BridgeCare, A Smart Connect System for the Homeless would incorporate, as a single web-based platform, all stakeholders (NGOs, donors, volunteers, hotels, healthcare providers, and welfare institutions) into a single interactive ecosystem. The system would allow and facilitate the donation of surplus food by hotels as well as individuals, provide transparent tracking of funds and resources, and ensure fair distribution of essentials. The uniqueness of BridgeCare lies in extending assistance not just to the homeless but also to orphanages and other needy children. This widens its reach and purpose. With immediate relief measures in place from food and shelter to medical assistance BridgeCare moves toward long-range empowerment through a host of education and awareness programs as well as skill development initiatives. The platform creates transparency and accountability among the stakeholders at all levels, thus reducing resource misuse while fostering trust. By connecting people in need to resources under the electronic BridgeCare promotes a sustainable flow of giving among all concerned. BridgeCare therefore is not just a technology project; it embodies humanitarian values striving to restore dignity, provide opportunity, and shape a society that overlooks no person in need.

title: Design and Optimization of a Compact Microstrip Patch Antenna for 24 GHz mmWave 5G Wireless Communications
authors: Ravi Joshi, Gaurav Kumar Soni, Dinesh Yadav, Rajni Idiwal, Monika Mathur
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The 5G wireless communications high-frequency waves in the millimeter-wave (mmWave) spectrum that provide ultra-fast data delivery, low latency, and high capacity are what antennas fall under. Therefore, among other futuristic ideas like driverless cars and high-speed wireless networks, it is essential to the realization of smart cities. In contemporary wireless applications, beamforming features and current-enabled multi-band operation are essential because they improve connectivity and increase data speed by reducing delays. Practically speaking, millimeter-wave communication is a key component of 5G, which will enable ultra-fast low latency support for innovations like intelligent homes, self-governing cars, and IoT-based entities in addition to augmented or virtual reality gaming. Faster yet reliable wireless communication triggers insatiable hunger among antenna designers who are strongly urged to keep developing efficient compact low-priced solutions responding effectively to these ever-evolving needs. The objective of this study is to enhance antenna performance and realize efficient 5G communication by designing a compact patch antenna optimized for mmWave wireless communication applications. The targeted frequency range for the planned antenna is centered around 24 GHz, with a frequency range of 23.804 GHz to 24.181 GHz. The antenna is designed on the Rogers RO3006 substrate with thickness h = 0.254 mm, as it exhibits low loss and high efficiency at mmWave frequencies. The patch antenna of proposed dimension of 10 × 10 × 0.258 mm3 is very compact, making it well-suited for communication devices that operate in the 24 GHz frequency band. The antenna can be used for 24 GHz mmWave wireless communication, 5G communication, radar systems, satellite communication and Industrial sensor applications.

title: A Review of Eco-Friendly Heating Furnaces using Renewable Energy Sources
authors: S. Arivazhagan, V. Geetha
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Generally induction furnace is used to melt ferrous and non-ferrous metals from the metal scraps. After producing the steel and alloys, they need to undergo a heat treatment process to enhance their mechanical properties, such as strength, stiffness, elasticity, plasticity, ductility, brittleness, malleability, toughness, resilience, creep, and hardness etc. The majority of heat treatment furnaces use electrical energy which is derived from burning of fossil fuels. The usage of abundant quantity of fossil fuels release greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and nitrous oxide (N2 O). Consequently, this intensifies the natural greenhouse effect. This leads to rising global warming and causes environmental effects. A detailed literature survey reveals that the traditional heat-treatment furnaces are polluting and not environmentally sustainable. A further scope of research has been identified based on the motivation of reducing the usage of fossil fuels and alleviating the effect of global warming by introducing a renewable energy-based heat treatment furnace integrated with power electronics converters for application in steel manufacturing industries.

title: Intensity Control in Y Branch Power Splitters using Structural Distortions in Photonic Band Gap (PBG) Lattice Structures
authors: Sheela D, Sarada Devi CH, Prameeladevi C, Bhavani N.P.G
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: A simple approach for intensity control in a PBG lattice structure based Y branch power splitter is presented in this paper. Field intensity is controlled by forming structural distortions through artificially created defective cells in the two dimensional lattice structures. The defective cells are formed by changing the dimensions of the cells placed at the specific locations in the crystal lattice structure. The defective cells are created at various edge locations and the corresponding output is measured. For analyzing these structures, finite difference time domain (FDTD) method is used. Output symmetry and power distribution at the two branches are observed under various configurations of these defective cells in order to assess the splitter's functionality. Three main patterns of defects are used and the Output is examined in terms of field distribution and frequency analysis. Every configuration has a distinct impact on energy transfer and mode coupling, providing valuable information for controlling the power splitting ratios between the branches. This paper provides a direct method to control the output as per the requirements and offers varying degrees of freedom to control the propagation of signals through the Y branch splitters.

title: High-Efficiency Double-Gate Adder/Subtractor using Emerging Device Technologies
authors: N.Vinod Kumar, F.Vincy Lloyd
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: The rapid evolution of semiconductor technology and the growing demand for energy-efficient digital systems have driven the need for high-performance arithmetic units with minimal power consumption. This work presents the implementation of power-efficient adder and Subtractor circuits utilizing Fin Field-Effect Transistors (FinFETs), a leading alternative to traditional CMOS devices in nano-scale design. FinFET technology offers superior control over short-channel effects, reduced leakage currents, and improved scalability, making it ideal for low-power applications. This work presents a double-gate FinFET-based high-performance adder and subtractor design for advanced arithmetic logic unit (ALU) applications. Traditional CMOS architectures are challenged by issues including short-channel effects and higher leakage currents when device size decreases. FinFET-based implementations of full adder and full subtractor circuits are proposed and examined to address these issues. Using double-gate FinFETs lowers power consumption and propagation delay while greatly improving electrostatic control and drive strength. Comparing the proposed FinFET adder and subtractor to their CMOS counterparts, simulation findings show that the former achieves up to 63.8% power reduction and 24.7% delay improvement, while the latter achieves 90.9% power reduction and 24.7% delay improvement. Due to these enhancements, the suggested architecture is ideal for high-speed, energy-efficient digital systems, especially ALUs used in portable and performance-critical applications.

title: Multi-Objective Control of High Temperature Electrolysis in a Microgrid
authors: Temitayo O. Olowu, Micah J. Casteel, Jan W. Lambrechtsen, Jeremy L. Hartvigsen
publish: 2026 IEEE Green Technologies Conference (GreenTech)
abstract: The growing penetration of intermittent energy resources and the evolving energy landscape necessitate enhanced flexibility in large controllable loads such as high-temperature electrolysis (HTE), particularly when integrated within microgrids (MGs). A central challenge in dynamic HTE operation lies in determining the optimal demand profile, which requires balancing ancillary service provision to the grid against maximizing hydrogen production to improve the capacity factor. To address this challenge, we propose a multiobjective optimization framework that enables autonomous Volt–Watt control of HTEs and the optimal dispatch of battery energy storage systems (BESS) within the microgrid. The objectives include improving the HTE capacity factor (HCF), minimizing voltage deviations, and reducing active power losses. A case study involving an operational HTE at Idaho National Laboratory (INL) and a representative MG configuration is evaluated using the proposed formulation. The Pareto-optimal solutions obtained demonstrate the effectiveness of the control strategy and highlight the trade-offs that can be systematically assessed by HTE owners and grid operators when determining the optimal HTE demand profile in coordination with a microgrid.

title: Code Generation with Small Language Models: A Codeforces-Based Study
authors: Débora Souza, Rohit Gheyi, Lucas Albuquerque, Gustavo Soares, Márcio Ribeiro
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate capabilities in code generation, potentially boosting developer productivity. However, their adoption remains limited by high computational costs, among other factors. Small Language Models (SLMs) present a lightweight alternative. While LLMs have been evaluated on competitive programming tasks, prior work often emphasizes metrics like Elo or pass rates, neglecting failure analysis. The potential of SLMs in this space remains underexplored. In this study, we benchmark three open SLMs—Llama-3.2-3B, Gemma-3-12B, and Phi-4-14B—across 280 Codeforces problems spanning Elo ratings from 800 to 2100 and covering 36 distinct topics. All models were tasked with generating Python solutions. Phi-4-14B achieved the best SLM performance with a pass@3 of 63.6%, nearing o3-mini-high (86.8%). Combining Python and C++ outputs increased Phi-4-14B’s pass@6 to 73.6%. A qualitative analysis revealed some failures stemmed from minor implementation issues rather than reasoning flaws.

title: Out-of-the-Box Uncertainty: Reducing Confident Errors with Dirichlet Classifiers
authors: Courtney Franzen, Farhad Pourkamali-Anaraki
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Overconfident misclassifications remain a critical barrier to deploying deep classifiers in risk-sensitive applications. This paper presents a targeted empirical comparison between two classification models: one trained with cross-entropy loss and temperature scaling (CE classifiers) and one trained using a Dirichlet-based surrogate loss with an annealed Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence penalty (Dirichlet classifiers). To support stable Dirichlet training in high-class-count regimes, we introduce a dataset-agnostic KL annealing schedule that mitigates training collapse, an essential step for tasks involving fine-grained recognition, multi-label categorization, or large taxonomies where class counts are high and reliable confidence estimation is critical. Evaluated across CIFAR-5 to CIFAR-100, Dirichlet classifiers consistently reduce high-confidence misclassifications by 2–5× compared to CE baselines, despite similar accuracy. To interpret these gains, we introduce the misclassification ratio curve and present a diagnostic panel combining confidence histograms, calibration curves, and threshold-based error metrics. Unlike CE classifiers, which require post hoc calibration to produce trustworthy confidence scores, Dirichlet models yield calibrated uncertainty directly from their output distributions. Our findings support Dirichlet classifiers as a strong alternative for uncertainty-aware applications, especially as class counts grow.

title: A Transformer-Based Cross-Platform Analysis of Public Discourse on the 15-Minute City Paradigm
authors: Gaurab Chhetri, Darrell Anderson, Boniphace Kutela, Subasish Das
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: This study presents the first multi-platform sentiment analysis of public opinion on the 15-minute city concept across Twitter, Reddit, and news media. Using compressed transformer models and Llama-3-8B for annotation, we classify sentiment across heterogeneous text domains. Our pipeline handles long-form and short-form text, supports consistent annotation, and enables reproducible evaluation. We benchmark five models (DistilRoBERTa, DistilBERT, MiniLM, ELECTRA, TinyBERT) using stratified 5-fold cross-validation, reporting F1-score, AUC, and training time. DistilRoBERTa achieved the highest F1 (0.8292), TinyBERT the best efficiency, and MiniLM the best cross-platform consistency. Results show News data yields inflated performance due to class imbalance, Reddit suffers from summarization loss, and Twitter offers moderate challenge. Compressed models perform competitively, challenging assumptions that larger models are necessary. We identify platformspecific trade-offs and propose directions for scalable, real-world sentiment classification in urban planning discourse.

title: From Answer to Origin: Page Number Grounding in Document-Level Question Answering
authors: Zarin T. Shejuti, Debzani Deb, Emily R. Dunkel
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: We introduce PageLocQA, a modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework tailored for page-level source attribution in long-document question answering. Rather than relying on complex agentic controllers or top-k retrieval alone, PageLocQA adopts a lightweight strategy that combines manual tool routing with structured prompting to generate semantically accurate answers along with page citations.We evaluate PageLocQA on a curated MODTRAN QA dataset, verified by domain experts, as well as benchmark datasets such as HotpotQA, TriviaQA, and Natural Questions. The MODTRAN 6 User Manual is a comprehensive technical manual detailing atmospheric radiative transfer modeling, input parameters, and configuration workflows, making it an ideal testbed for page-grounded QA. While it does not outperform large-scale frameworks like ChatQA or SelfRAG, PageLocQA achieves competitive BERTScore results across all datasets and demonstrates superior grounding performance on MODTRAN, achieving a BERTScore of 0.871 and F1 score of 0.303.These findings underscore the practicality of lightweight RAG-based approaches in settings that require both accuracy and explainability, especially in resource-constrained or scientific QA environments.

title: Real-Time Video Frame De-Duplication Using Hybrid Model for Surveillance Video Change Detection
authors: Bogem Chaithanya Srinivas, Arivoli A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Contemporary surveillance cameras produce a huge amount of video content, much of which represents static scenes with little motion. To address this, we propose a deep learning framework for temporal difference detection, with a focus on intelligent video de-duplication. The main strength of our method lies in modeling temporal variations. For this purpose, attention modules are applied hierarchically across four encoder layers, enhanced with multi-scale attention and channel recalibration. Our method employs a Siamese network with a shared-weight encoder that computes frame differences using learnable combinations of absolute and squared differences. In addition, multiscale attention is applied with kernel sizes of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 1,3 \times 3,5 \times 5$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 \times 7$</tex>. The decoder uses transposed convolution for upsampling, skip connections for feature fusion, and deep supervision at three intermediate points. Experiments on the CDNet2014 dataset achieve an F1 score of 0.8429. This provides a foundation for video de-duplication in surveillance systems.

title: Comparative Analysis of Passive Noise Cancellation Using LMS, NLMS, RLS, and APA Algorithms
authors: Abhiram Rajesh, Abin O K, Akshay Gopal K, Amal Vinod, Anupam Krishna, Karthik S Unnithan, Anoop S
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: This work explores adaptive noise cancellation using Least Mean Squares (LMS), Normalized Least Mean Squares (NLMS), Recursive Least Squares (RLS), and Affine Projection Algorithms (APA) under a common framework. A practical signal model is used in which the desired audio is contaminated by correlated noise, while a separate reference channel provides noise-only information. To ensure a fair comparison, all algorithms are tested with the same filter settings and input conditions. Their performance is evaluated through learning behavior, weight stability, and frame-based Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) analysis. The results show that LMS is simple but converges slowly, NLMS improves stability, RLS delivers the fastest convergence and best noise suppression, and APA offers a balanced compromise for correlated noise scenarios.

title: Reliability Study of Thermal Inversion Effect on Ultra Low Power Array Multiplier Using 7nm FinFET Technology for 3D IC Integration
authors: Shilagani Shivaprasad, MD Badhesha, J. Ajayan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Multipliers are indispensable in signal processing, AI and cryptographic applications, demanding architectures that achieve both ultra-low power operation and high computational speed. This work analyses <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 4$</tex> array multipliers implemented with Mirror-Full-Adder (MFA) and Hybrid-Full-Adder (HFA) in 7 nm FinFET technology, where FinFET devices provide new opportunities to achieve this balance between performance and energy efficiency. Gate length scaling (20 nm-7 nm), voltage variation (1.2-0.7 V), and thermal conditions \left(27-107° C\right) are jointly evaluated to study delay, power, and leakage behavior. Results highlight the thermal inversion effect (TIE), where higher temperatures reduce delay but increase dynamic power, reflecting the speedpower trade-off. Then, in conjunction with the thermal reliability assessment, the short-circuit power and leakage current analysis both provide credibility to the robustness of FinFETs, confirming that switching activity is more thermally sensitive than leakage. More broadly, this work provides usable design knowledge to be used for an efficient selection of full-adder architectures used in FinFET-based multiplier designs and future computing technologies, enabling the incorporation of deeply sub-nanometer arithmetic circuits for advanced FET-based applications.

title: Formation of Competencies of Future Engineers in the Digital Economy: Integration of Business-Oriented Scenarios Into Educational Laboratories of Electrical Engineering and Electronics
authors: Irina A. Frank, Galina P. Platunina, Tatyana Y. Salutina, Milana T. Benia
publish: 2026 Systems of Signals Generating and Processing in the Field of on Board Communications
abstract: In the context of the transformation of the economy towards digital technologies and industry 4.0, the need for engineering personnel with not only technical, but also business-oriented competencies is increasing. The article presents an approach to the integration of business scenarios into educational laboratories as a mechanism for the formation of interdisciplinary competencies among future engineers. A methodology for designing simulation laboratory work based on real-world industrial applications is proposed. The integration of business-oriented scenarios into the laboratory practice helps to reduce the gap between academic education and the requirements of the labor market, and also increases the motivation of students by applying knowledge in the context of real production tasks. The proposed approach can be adapted for other engineering fields and serves as the basis for the formation of a digital transformation of engineering education.

title: Cyber-Physical Testbed with SOC Integration for Evaluating DERMS Security Using NS-3 and SunSpec Modbus
authors: Ishan Prashant Pathak, Nuri Yilmazer
publish: 2026 IEEE Green Technologies Conference (GreenTech)
abstract: The increasing deployment of distributed energy resources (DERs), including photovoltaic systems, wind turbines, and battery energy storage systems, introduces new reliability and security challenges for Distributed Energy Resource Management Systems (DERMS) operating over communication networks. This paper presents a reproducible, containerized cyber-physical testbed for evaluating DERMS supervisory control under realistic communication impairments. The platform integrates NS-3 packet-level network emulation with SunSpec-compliant DER emulators (300 kW PV, 150 kW wind, 100 kW/200 kWh storage) on an IEEE 13-bus feeder model, with passive intrusion detection supporting four documented attack scenarios. All specifications are configuration-driven, enabling repeatable experimentation without hardware dependencies. Experimental results demonstrate 86.52% application-layer packet delivery ratio under 10% configured link-layer loss, 45 ms average delay, 56 ms control response times, and detection of all injected attacks with zero false positives. The primary contribution is the testbed architecture a Docker-based platform supporting integrated evaluation of DERMS control, communication resilience, and security monitoring based on application-level outcomes rather than network metrics alone.

title: Hybrid Hydropower Power Plant Contributions to Grid Resilience During Controlled Islanding Events
authors: Abiodun Oseni, Thabiso Mabote, Ted K.A. Brekken, Eduardo Cotilla-Sanchez
publish: 2026 IEEE Green Technologies Conference (GreenTech)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable and inverter-based resources has heightened the need for resilient power system operation under severe disturbances. This paper presents a hierarchical controlled islanding framework for transmission networks that explicitly accounts for heterogeneous generation dynamics, including hydropower and battery energy storage systems. The framework is evaluated on the IEEE 39-bus system under severe delayed-clearing faults. Comparative studies are conducted for two scenarios: a power system with a hydroturbine-equipped generator and a hybrid hydrotur-bine–energy storage plant through the integration of Inverter-Based Resources. Results demonstrate that while conventional hydro-turbine dynamics influence intra-island behavior, the integration of battery energy storage significantly improves frequency response and enables the formation of larger, dynamically stable islands. The result also shows that integration of a hybrid-hydroturbine system in Scenario B increases the amount of load sustained by 17% when compared to Scenario A with a stand-alone hydro-system.

title: Optimizing Natural Language Processing Architectures via Metaheuristic Search Strategies
authors: Priyanka Kaushik, Dheer Singh, Sudhakar Polasi, Priyanka Rani, Rahul Pradhan, Javalkar Dinesh Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: The models and the process of deep neural networks (DNNs) and machine learning (ML) used in natural language processing (NLP) usually entail many computational needs and elaborate design, thus searching and optimizing them may be a difficult task. The paper has indicated that metaheuristic techniques can be used to enhance the performance of NLP models with respect to optimal parameter pruning. Genetic Algorithms (GA), Swarm Intelligence (SI), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Monarch Butterfly Optimization (MBO) have demonstrated excellent performances in tuning hyperparameters, feature selection, and optimization of the general model efficiency. Recent works have given particular importance to the hybrid approaches, including the integration of GA and autoencoders, which have significantly reinforced CNN-based feature extraction. In addition, the effect of multi-population PSO (MPPSO) on enhancing model diversity in performing a task such as software defect detection with the help of TF-IDF and BERT representations is also analyzed. In spite of these developments, there are still issues such as lack of datasets, reliance on pre processing techniques, and differences in the performance of metaheuristics. Future studies ought to examine the combination of state-of-the-art deep learning architectures, including transformers and GANs, and metaheuristic-based pruning to gain higher efficiency, flexibility, and scalability of NLP.

title: Privacy-Enhancing Visual Analytics: Homomorphic and Encrypted Inference for Smart Cameras
authors: S. Sree Hari Raju, M. Sonia, L Suresh Kumar, Jatin Arora
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
abstract: Smart camera systems increasingly rely on cloud-based visual analytics, yet transmitting raw video or intermediate features exposes sensitive visual information and raises serious privacy concerns. Existing privacy-preserving inference techniques either provide insufficient protection or incur prohibitive computational and latency overhead, limiting their practical deployment in real-world camera networks. This study aimed to design and evaluate an effective, end to end privacy-sensitive visual analytics system that will allow inference of smart cameras accurately with a high level of cryptographic protection. An experimental study was conducted using publicly available multimodal image, video, and audio-visual datasets. The proposed framework is a mix of a distilled InternVideo2 backbone, packed CKKS homomorphic encryption and selective TFHE-based non-linear evaluation and is compared to encrypted-feature and partial HE baselines based on accuracy, latency, throughput, communication, energy, and privacy utility metrics. Experimental results demonstrates that proposed framework achieves higher accuracy in the performed tasks than the baseline and hybrid models, reaching the higher improvement of up to 0.8% of object detection mAP and 0.9% accuracy of action recognition. The framework is smaller (by about 65 percent) than encrypted-feature inference, has smaller communication overhead (more than 70%) and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times$</tex> increase in encrypted inference throughput and lower end-to-end latency. These results demonstrate that fully encrypted smart camera analytics can achieve near-plaintext accuracy with practical computational and energy costs when model architecture and cryptographic execution are jointly optimized. The proposed framework will bring the idea of privacy-conscious visual analytics a step closer to being made available in sensitive and controlled settings.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy Lesion Segmentation through Attention Mechanisms
authors: Aruna Jithesh, Chinmayi Karumuri, Venkata Kiran Reddy Kotha, Meghana Doddapuneni, Taehee Jeong
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is an eye disease which arises due to diabetes mellitus. It might cause vision loss and blindness. To prevent irreversible vision loss, early detection through systematic screening is crucial. Although researchers have developed numerous automated deep learning-based algorithms for DR screening, their clinical applicability remains limited, particularly in lesion segmentation. Our method provides pixel-level annotations for lesions, which practically supports Ophthalmologist to screen DR from fundus images. In this work, we segmented four types of DR-related lesions: microaneurysms, soft exudates, hard exudates, and hemorrhages on 757 images from DDR dataset. To enhance lesion segmentation, an attention mechanism was integrated with DeepLab-V3+. Compared to the baseline model, the Attention-DeepLab model increases mean average precision (mAP) from 0.3010 to 0.3326 and the mean Intersection over Union (IoU) from 0.1791 to 0.1928. The model also increased microaneurysm detection from 0.0205 to 0.0763, a clinically significant improvement. The detection of microaneurysms is the earliest visible symptom of DR. Our code is available at https://github.com/taeheej/Diabetic-Retinopathy-Lesion-Segmentation-through-Attention-Mechanisms.

title: Smart Clinic: A New Innovation in Telemedicine
authors: Nivedha.D, S. Nagarajan, C.Rohith Bhat, M.Sujatha, M.Moorthi, M.Madhu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: Telemedicine is revolutionizing healthcare by enabling remote consultations through interactive audiovisual media. The proposed system provides basic counseling to patients and automatically dispenses medication for minor ailments, following consultation with a doctor via network connections. Unfortunately, many rural areas face a shortage of medical professionals, leading to concerns about healthcare quality and staffing. To address this, our system checks patients' vital signs, such as temperature and heartbeat, and offers voice-based counseling in local languages. The computer then analyzes the data and provides a diagnosis, which is sent to a doctor for review. Based on the doctor's prescription, the machine either dispenses medication or advises the patient to visit a hospital.

title: Automated Consistency Monitoring System For Optimal Jaggery Production
authors: R.Sureshkumar, S.J.SujiPrasad, S.Dhaneshwara, P.Dharshini, A.Mohan Sanjay, M.S.Aravinth
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC)
abstract: To make good-quality jaggery, it is important to control the properties of sugarcane juice very carefully. Traditional methods take a lot of time and need people to watch and control the process by hand, which often leads to mistakes. This paper explains a system that uses automation to improve the jaggery making process. An ESP32 microcontroller is used to control everything. An ultrasonic sensor checks how much juice is in the tank, and the total amount is calculated based on the tank’s size. A pH sensor monitors the juice acidity, and based on the pH value, a motor driven lime addition mechanism adjusts the juice pH appropriately. After pH correction, the juice is automatically transferred to a second tank for boiling. Stirring and heating processes are controlled based on temperature measurements, and when the juice reaches the striking point temperature, the system stops heating and stirring process automatically, a buzzer is triggered to indicate the completion of process. Every stage in the process is continuously updated and displayed on an LCD screen. The proposed system reduces manual intervention, improves process accuracy, and enhances overall jaggery quality.

title: Comparative Study of Static Slicing and Overflow Based Resources Sharing for VoIP Oriented Network
authors: Sergey N. Stepanov, Knaj Nouma, Irina V. Stepanova, Tammam Z. Dawood, Margarita G. Kanishcheva
publish: 2026 Systems of Signals Generating and Processing in the Field of on Board Communications
abstract: Efficient resource allocation is a key challenge in multiservice networks that simultaneously support both real-time voice traffic and elastic data services. Voice over IP (VoIP) traffic requires strict quality-of-service guarantees, particularly in terms of call loss probability, while file traffic can tolerate higher levels of delays. This paper presents a comparative study of two resource allocation strategies for VoIP-oriented networks: static resource slicing and overflow-based resource sharing. In the static slicing approach, network resources are partitioned between voice and data traffic, ensuring strict isolation but leading to potential wastage of capacity. The overflow-based approach allows voice traffic to access additional shared resources under overload conditions while maintaining priority over data traffic. Therefore, the overflow-based approach was modeled as a continuous Markov chain (CTMC) with state-dependent transitions. The steady state probabilities were obtained from global balance equations. Several metrics describing node performance were derived. Performance evaluation results demonstrate that both approaches achieve comparable VoIP loss performance at the target quality level, but the overflow-based strategy significantly reduces the total number of required channels, improving overall resource utilization.

title: Spatial Discrete Sampling-Based Near-Field Beamforming for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces
authors: Dejun Ma, Yuxuan Ma, Jiaying Sun, Lijia Jiao
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Neural Networks, Information and Communication Engineering (NNICE)
abstract: In 6G wireless systems, large-scale reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) with thousands of elements are expected to play a key role. Due to the extremely large aperture, most users fall into the near-field region of the RIS, where the conventional far-field plane-wave assumption no longer holds. As a result, applying traditional far-field codebooks leads to severe performance degradation because of the serious model mismatch. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes an efficient near-field beam optimization framework for large-scale RISs. Specifically, a near-field codebook that accurately matches the spherical wavefront is constructed by conducting three-dimensional discrete spatial sampling over the target region. Furthermore, a low-complexity hierarchical search algorithm is proposed, which decomposes the high-dimensional global optimization into successive fine-grained local searches, not only significantly reducing the beam training overhead but also effectively optimizing the user signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR). Simulation results verify that the proposed scheme delivers superior user rate performance over far-field codebook-based beamforming across different transmit powers and RIS unit counts, maintains prominent advantages in typical near-field distances, and achieves near-optimal performance overall, though its user rate is slightly inferior to that of near-field exhaustive search due to inter-layer error propagation in hierarchical search.
